title: The Water Cure
passage:
For a decade the little town of Marish Wells sold nothing but its water. When a Doctor Aldous Frane published his pamphlet on the spring's supposed cures in 1841, visitors arrived by the coachload; three hotels went up in as many years, and the pump room was enlarged twice. What the town's promoters never advertised was that the same railway they had lobbied so hard to attract, and which finally reached them in 1849, could carry a convalescent just as easily onward to the coast. Within a decade the grander guests were taking the cure abroad or by the sea, and the hotels of Marish Wells were letting their rooms to commercial travellers. The spring had not changed; the map around it had.
— from a county history by Selwyn Marchant

questions.prompt: What does the text imply about the railway that reached Marish Wells?

options.text: It finally arrived in 1849, several years after the town's first hotels had been built.
It reduced the town's fortunes by fouling the water of its celebrated mineral spring.
It proved to visitors that the spring's much-advertised cures had never worked at all.
The line the town had fought to attract also made it easy for its visitors to travel elsewhere.

questions.rationale: TYPE-002 inference, one logical inch beyond the text. The passage notes the promoters never advertised that 'the same railway they had lobbied so hard to attract ... could carry a convalescent just as easily onward to the coast', then reports the grander guests leaving for the sea and abroad; the implication is that the sought-after railway helped drain the town of the very visitors it was meant to bring. D states exactly that inference. A = too_literal: a true, directly stated fact (arrival date, hotels already built), which is retrieval, not the implication the stem asks for. B = contradiction/outside_knowledge: the text says 'The spring had not changed', so no fouling of the water is implied. C = too_far/two-step leap: 'supposed cures' signals the writer's mild scepticism, but the text never says the cures were exposed as worthless, and the decline is attributed to the map, not to any debunking. Only D is a defensible implication.
